slides.title: Exploring Large Numbers
slides.text: Let's learn about whole numbers up to ten thousand. These numbers have four digits and help us count large collections of objects in daily life. In math, we express this as: Numbers up to 10,000 = big values. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. Can you think of a real-world collection that has thousands of items?

examples.title: Example 1
examples.content: Review standard details: Numbers up to 10,000 = big values.

slides.title: Visualizing Thousands
slides.text: We can visualize numbers using base-ten blocks. A large block represents one thousand, which is made of ten flat hundreds blocks joined together. In math, we express this as: 1000 = 10 hundreds blocks. . . . . . . How many tens blocks do we need to make one thousand blocks?

examples.title: Example 2
examples.content: Review standard details: 1000 = 10 hundreds blocks.

slides.title: The Four-Digit Place Value Chart
slides.text: We organize four-digit numbers in a place value chart. The positions from right to left are Ones, Tens, Hundreds, and Thousands. In math, we express this as: Place values = Thousands, Hundreds, Tens, Ones. . . . . . . Which digit in the number 4,567 is in the thousands place?

examples.title: Example 3
examples.content: Review standard details: Place values = Thousands, Hundreds, Tens, Ones.

slides.title: Understanding Place Value
slides.text: Place value is the position of a digit in a number. It tells us if the digit represents ones, tens, hundreds, or thousands. In math, we express this as: Place value of 5 in 5,432 = Thousands. . . . . . . What is the place value of the digit 3 in the number 8,312?

examples.title: Example 4
examples.content: Review standard details: Place value of 5 in 5,432 = Thousands.

slides.title: Calculating the Value of a Digit
slides.text: The value of a digit is found by multiplying the digit by its place value. For example, a 6 in the hundreds place has a value of 600. In math, we express this as: Value of 6 in 7,621 = 6 x 100 = 600. . . . . . What is the value of the digit 4 in the number 4,921?

examples.title: Example 5
examples.content: Review standard details: Value of 6 in 7,621 = 6 x 100 = 600.

slides.title: Reading Numbers in Words
slides.text: When reading a number, we read the thousands place, say the word thousand, and then read the remaining three digits as a standard number. In math, we express this as: 4,520 = four thousand, five hundred twenty. . . . . . . How do you read the number 7,809 in words aloud?

examples.title: Example 6
examples.content: Review standard details: 4,520 = four thousand, five hundred twenty.

slides.title: Writing Numbers in Numerals
slides.text: When writing a number, we write the digits in their correct place value positions. We use a comma to separate the thousands from the hundreds. In math, we express this as: Three thousand, forty = 3,040. . . . . . . How do you write six thousand, five hundred twelve in numerals?

examples.title: Example 7
examples.content: Review standard details: Three thousand, forty = 3,040.

slides.title: The Expanded Form
slides.text: Expanded form shows a number written as the sum of the values of its digits. This helps us see the breakdown of thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. In math, we express this as: 5,234 = 5,000 + 200 + 30 + 4. . . . . . . Can you write the number 8,740 in expanded form?

examples.title: Example 8
examples.content: Review standard details: 5,234 = 5,000 + 200 + 30 + 4.

slides.title: Pineapple Harvesting counts
slides.text: Farmers in Polomolok count their harvested sweet pineapples. A small farm plot can produce four thousand, five hundred pineapples in one week. In math, we express this as: Harvest = 4,500 pineapples in Polomolok. . . . . . . If a farmer harvests 4,500 pineapples, how many thousands are there?

examples.title: Example 9
examples.content: Review standard details: Harvest = 4,500 pineapples in Polomolok.

slides.title: Lake Sebu Tourist Tracking
slides.text: The tourism office in Lake Sebu tracks the number of visitors. Last month, eight thousand, one hundred twenty-five tourists visited the scenic waterfalls. In math, we express this as: Visitors = 8,125 tourists at waterfalls. . . . . . . How do you write the tourist number 8,125 in words?

examples.title: Example 10
examples.content: Review standard details: Visitors = 8,125 tourists at waterfalls.

slides.title: Barangay Population Records
slides.text: Barangay records in South Cotabato help us track resident counts. A local barangay has nine thousand, four hundred thirty residents living in the community. In math, we express this as: Population = 9,430 residents in South Cotabato. . . . . . . What is the value of the digit 9 in the population 9,430?

examples.title: Example 11
examples.content: Review standard details: Population = 9,430 residents in South Cotabato.

slides.title: What is Rounding?
slides.text: Rounding is finding a simpler number close to the actual value. It makes numbers easier to use and estimate in daily math problems. In math, we express this as: Rounding = finding the nearest benchmark number. . . . . . . Why do we round numbers when estimating total costs?

examples.title: Example 12
examples.content: Review standard details: Rounding = finding the nearest benchmark number.

slides.title: Rounding to the Nearest Ten
slides.text: To round to the nearest ten, we look at the ones digit. If it is 5 or more, we round up; if it is less than 5, we round down. In math, we express this as: 4,527 rounded to tens = 4,530. . . . . . . What is 3,412 rounded to the nearest ten?

examples.title: Example 13
examples.content: Review standard details: 4,527 rounded to tens = 4,530.

slides.title: Rounding to the Nearest Hundred
slides.text: To round to the nearest hundred, we check the tens digit. Since 8 is 5 or more, we round the hundreds digit up to the next hundred. In math, we express this as: 6,781 rounded to hundreds = 6,800. . . . . . . What is 5,230 rounded to the nearest hundred?

examples.title: Example 14
examples.content: Review standard details: 6,781 rounded to hundreds = 6,800.

slides.title: Rounding to the Nearest Thousand
slides.text: To round to the nearest thousand, we check the hundreds digit. Since 2 is less than 5, we round down, keeping the thousands digit unchanged. In math, we express this as: 8,245 rounded to thousands = 8,000. . . . . . . What is 7,850 rounded to the nearest thousand?

examples.title: Example 15
examples.content: Review standard details: 8,245 rounded to thousands = 8,000.

slides.title: Comparing Four-Digit Numbers
slides.text: To compare two numbers, we look at the digits from left to right. We start with the thousands, then hundreds, tens, and ones. In math, we express this as: 8,450 vs 8,290: 8,450 is larger. . . . . . . Which number is larger: 5,670 or 5,690?

examples.title: Example 16
examples.content: Review standard details: 8,450 vs 8,290: 8,450 is larger.

slides.title: Using Comparison Symbols
slides.text: We use relation symbols to show comparison: greater than (>), less than (<), and equal to (=). The open side of the symbol points to the larger number. In math, we express this as: 4,560 > 4,520. . . . . . . If the checking digit is five or more, we round up; if it is less than five, we round down. Which symbol fits in the blank: 8,901 ___ 8,910?

examples.title: Example 17
examples.content: Review standard details: 4,560 > 4,520.

slides.title: Ordering Numbers: Smallest to Largest
slides.text: Ordering in ascending order means listing numbers from the lowest value to the highest. We compare each number to find its correct position. In math, we express this as: 3,120 < 3,450 < 3,890. . . . . . . Arrange these numbers in ascending order: 4,500, 4,200, 4,800.

examples.title: Example 18
examples.content: Review standard details: 3,120 < 3,450 < 3,890.

slides.title: Ordering Numbers: Largest to Smallest
slides.text: Ordering in descending order means listing numbers from the highest value to the lowest. We find the largest number first and write them down. In math, we express this as: 9,800 > 9,500 > 9,100. . . . . . . Arrange these numbers in descending order: 6,100, 6,700, 6,400.

examples.title: Example 19
examples.content: Review standard details: 9,800 > 9,500 > 9,100.

slides.title: Finding Number Patterns
slides.text: We can count in patterns of tens, hundreds, or thousands. Recognizing patterns helps us count large quantities of items very quickly. In math, we express this as: Pattern: 1,000, 2,000, 3,000, 4,000. . . . . . . What is the next number in the pattern: 3,500, 4,500, 5,500?

examples.title: Example 20
examples.content: Review standard details: Pattern: 1,000, 2,000, 3,000, 4,000.

slides.title: Zero as a Placeholder
slides.text: Zero is a vital placeholder in our number system. It shows that a place value has no value, keeping other digits in their correct positions. In math, we express this as: 4,050 vs 4,500. . . . . . . Why would a number change if we removed the zero placeholder?

examples.title: Example 21
examples.content: Review standard details: 4,050 vs 4,500.

slides.title: Counting Philippine Pesos
slides.text: We use large numbers when counting money. If you save five ₱1,000 bills, four ₱100 bills, and one ₱50 bill, you have ₱5,450. In math, we express this as: Money = ₱5,450 total savings. . . . . . . How do you write five thousand, four hundred fifty Pesos in numerals?

examples.title: Example 22
examples.content: Review standard details: Money = ₱5,450 total savings.

slides.title: Common Error: Writing Placeholders
slides.text: A common error is forgetting zeros when writing numbers. For example, writing five thousand, three as 53 instead of the correct 5,003. In math, we express this as: Five thousand, three = 5,003. . . . . . . Why is it incorrect to write five thousand, three as 53?

examples.title: Example 23
examples.content: Review standard details: Five thousand, three = 5,003.

slides.title: Rounding and Comparing Problems
slides.text: Let's solve a problem. A school has 4,520 students. Round this number to the nearest thousand. Since the hundreds digit is 5, we round up to 5,000. In math, we express this as: Round 4,520 to nearest thousand = 5,000. . . . . . . Is 8,500 rounded to the nearest thousand 8,000 or 9,000?

examples.title: Example 24
examples.content: Review standard details: Round 4,520 to nearest thousand = 5,000.

slides.title: Summary of Week 4 Lessons
slides.text: Let's review: we learned to represent, read, and write numbers up to ten thousand, identify place values, round to tens/hundreds/thousands, and compare/order numbers. In math, we express this as: Numbers to 10,000 = read, write, compare, round. . . . . . . How do large numbers help us record statistics in South Cotabato?

examples.title: Example 25
examples.content: Review standard details: Numbers to 10,000 = read, write, compare, round.

challenge.q: I am a 4-digit number. My thousands digit is 3. My hundreds digit is 0. My tens digit is 5. My ones digit is 2. What number am I?
I am a 4-digit number. My thousands digit is 4. My hundreds digit is 0. My tens digit is 5. My ones digit is 2. What number am I?
I am a 4-digit number. My thousands digit is 5. My hundreds digit is 0. My tens digit is 5. My ones digit is 2. What number am I?
I am a 4-digit number. My thousands digit is 6. My hundreds digit is 0. My tens digit is 5. My ones digit is 2. What number am I?
I am a 4-digit number. My thousands digit is 7. My hundreds digit is 0. My tens digit is 5. My ones digit is 2. What number am I?

pages:
Exercise 1: Base-Ten Block Values

Write the number represented by the base-ten block models below.

1. 3 thousands blocks, 5 hundreds sheets, 2 tens rods, and 8 ones blocks =
3528
2. 7 thousands blocks, 0 hundreds sheets, 4 tens rods, and 0 ones blocks =
7040

Exercise 2: Writing Numerals

Write the correct standard numeral for the given number in words.

1. "Five thousand four hundred ninety-two" in numerals:
5492

Exercise 3: Place Value up to 10,000

Identify the place value of the underlined digit.

1. In the number 8,345, what is the place value of the underlined digit 3?
hundreds

performance.title: Tuna Harvest Record Block Drawing
performance.desc: Represent the number 4,325 (which represents boxes of tuna harvested at the General Santos City port) using drawings of base-ten blocks. Draw 4 large cubes for thousands, 3 flat squares for hundreds, 2 vertical rectangles for tens, and 5 small dots for ones. Color them neatly, write the number in standard form and in words, take a photo, and upload it.
labels: Number written in standard form and in words
